compliance.gentle_note: This guide covers the stones most often sold as imitations. Home tests help you spot common fakes, but for valuable specimens (jadeite, moldavite, untreated turquoise), a gemological lab report is the gold standard. Crystal meanings and energetic properties are separate from authenticity.

content:
By Earthward Editorial Team · Earthward's crystal care and sourcing team · Updated 2026-06-28 · Reviewed by Earthward's mineral-sourcing reviewers

Some crystals are faked far more often than others — and knowing which ones, and how each is typically imitated, is the single most useful skill a buyer can build. This guide covers the ten crystals most commonly sold as fakes, with the mineral data and the one headline test that catches each imitation. If a stone you are considering is on this list, read its section before you buy.

Quick Answer: How to Tell If a Crystals Is Real

The fakes cluster around a predictable set of stones: those that are popular, expensive enough to make substitution profitable, or visually easy to copy in glass, plastic, or dyed alternatives. For each of the ten stones below, there is one test that catches most fakes — the matrix test for turquoise, the pyrite-fleck test for lapis, the heft test for jadeite, the origin test for moldavite. The general tests from the full guide (bubbles, temperature, hardness, price, naming) apply across all of them.

The three most reliable at-home tests to start with:

  • Naming and price — read the listing first. Vague names ("purple crystal," "energy stone") and prices far below the species' market range are the strongest pre-purchase warnings.
  • Bubbles under a loupe — for any transparent stone on this list (amethyst, citrine, clear quartz, rose quartz), round bubbles mean glass.
  • Species-specific tests — each stone has one headline signal (turquoise: matrix, lapis: pyrite, jadeite: heft, malachite: banding). Learn the one that matters for the stone you want.

No single home test is 100% conclusive — these tests work together. For valuable specimens, a gemological lab report is the gold standard.

Why Fake Crystals Are So Common

The fake-crystal market is not random — it concentrates where the economics favor substitution. A stone gets heavily faked when three conditions line up: it is popular enough that demand outpaces ethical supply, it is expensive enough (or its high grades are expensive enough) that substitution is profitable, and its appearance can be approximated by glass, plastic, dye, or a cheaper mineral. The ten stones below meet all three conditions. Together they account for the great majority of fakes a buyer will encounter.

The fakes follow recognizable patterns. Glass imitations dominate for transparent stones (amethyst, citrine, clear quartz, rose quartz) — dyed to match the color, often with round bubbles visible under a loupe. Plastic and resin fakes appear in opaque stones sold as cheap beads (turquoise, malachite, lapis). Dyed stones substitute a cheap mineral for an expensive one: howlite dyed blue for turquoise, jasper dyed for lapis, low-grade quartz dyed for jade. Lab-grown (synthetic) quartz is chemically genuine but factory-made. And for rare stones like moldavite, plain green bottle glass is the most common imitation, relying on buyers who do not know the origin story.

Each stone has a specific weakness that fakes exploit and a specific test that catches the fake. Turquoise's weakness is that dyed howlite copies its color; its test is the natural matrix. Lapis's weakness is that dyed jasper copies its blue; its test is the pyrite flecks. Jadeite's weakness is its high value, inviting quartz and serpentine substitutes; its test is the density. Moldavite's weakness is its rarity and high price; its test is the Czech-only origin. The general tests from the full guide apply everywhere, but the species-specific tests are what catch the sophisticated fakes for each stone.

How Fakes Are Made (and the Tests That Catch Them)

Before the stone-by-stone breakdown, here is how the most common fakes are made and the general tests that catch them. The seven methods below apply across all ten stones; each stone's section then adds the one test specific to that species. The mineral data table that follows gives you the numbers — Mohs hardness, specific gravity, refractive index — to verify what the tests suggest.

🏷️ Test 10: Naming & Source Transparency

The science behind it: Reputable sellers identify stones by their proper mineral name and origin, because mineral species and locality affect value and care. Vague or misleading names are a marketing red flag: the glass industry calls leaded glass "crystal" (lead crystal, crystal glass), so "crystal quartz" can mean glass rather than natural quartz. Sellers who hide behind "mystery stone," "energy crystal," or omit locality are harder to hold accountable.

Real vs fake at a glance:

SignalGenuineFake (glass / plastic)
Stone name on listingProper mineral name (Natural Amethyst, SiO₂)Vague ("purple crystal," "energy stone") or industry code ("crystal quartz" = glass)
Treatment disclosureDisclosed (heat-treated citrine, lab-created)Hidden or denied
LocalitySpecific origin (Brazil, Uruguay, Zambia)Generic ("imported," none listed)

How to do it:

  1. Read the listing carefully — does it name the actual mineral species, or use vague marketing terms?
  2. Check for treatment disclosure: reputable sellers note heat treatment, irradiation, dyeing, or lab creation.
  3. Look for locality information — specific origins (Brazil for amethyst, Madagascar for labradorite) signal a seller who knows their stock.

Limitations: A seller who names stones correctly can still sell glass; and a small honest seller may omit locality without ill intent. Naming transparency is a positive signal but not proof. Conversely, vague naming is a warning, not a verdict. Use it to decide which sellers warrant deeper trust, combined with the other tests.

Best stones for this test: All stones, especially when buying online sight-unseen. The single most useful pre-purchase signal.

Source: GIA / FTC guidelines on gemstone disclosure.

💰 Test 9: Price Red Flag

The science behind it: Genuine natural crystals carry real costs: mining, sorting, cutting, polishing, transport, and the reality that high-quality specimens are finite. A price far below the established market range signals that something is off — either synthetic, glass, dyed, or reconstituted material being sold as natural. While promotions exist, a consistent dramatic discount across a seller's entire catalog is a structural red flag, not a sale.

Real vs fake at a glance:

SignalGenuineFake (glass / plastic)
Price vs. market rangeWithin reasonable market range for the species and qualityFar below market (often 50-80% cheaper)
Consistency of discountOccasional promotions on specific piecesEntire catalog suspiciously cheap
Quality claims vs. pricePrice matches claimed grade"Museum quality" at bargain prices

How to do it:

  1. Compare the seller's prices against established reference points (gemological market guides, reputable dealers).
  2. Be wary of "too good to be true" pricing — a large natural amethyst geode for the price of a tumbled stone is not a deal, it is a different material.
  3. Use price as one signal among many — a fair price does not guarantee authenticity, but a wildly low price is a strong warning.

Limitations: Price alone never confirms authenticity — a seller can charge full price for glass. And genuine inexpensive stones exist (clear quartz, common agate, rose quartz rough are legitimately affordable). Use price as an auxiliary signal: a red flag warrants deeper testing, but a reasonable price does not exempt a stone from the other tests.

Best stones for this test: All stones, as an auxiliary signal — especially high-value species (jadeite, moldavite, large amethyst geodes, tanzanite) where prices are well-established and dramatic discounts are implausible.

Source: market reference — gemological pricing guides.

🔍 Test 4: Bubble Inspection (10x Loupe)

The science behind it: Manufactured glass traps small spherical or teardrop-shaped air bubbles as it cools — a tell-tale sign of industrial origin. Natural minerals form over geological time and instead contain natural inclusions: needle-like rutile, two-phase gas-liquid inclusions, mineral crystals, or "fingerprints" of healed fractures. A 10x jeweler's loupe reveals the difference: round bubbles mean glass; irregular inclusions mean natural.

Real vs fake at a glance:

SignalGenuineFake (glass / plastic)
Under 10x loupeNeedle/plate inclusions, growth lines, mineral flecksPerfectly round bubbles, swirls, no irregular inclusions
Bubble shapeNone (natural minerals rarely trap air)Spherical or teardrop bubbles
Internal textureChaotic, asymmetric, "natural"Too clean, or with flow lines from manufacturing

How to do it:

  1. Use a 10x jeweler's loupe (an inexpensive tool — about $10-15 online).
  2. Hold the stone against a light source (window or lamp) and look through it with the loupe.
  3. Scan for perfectly round, spherical bubbles — these are characteristic of glass. Natural stones show irregular inclusions instead.

Limitations: High-quality optical glass and fused quartz can be manufactured bubble-free, so the absence of bubbles does not guarantee natural origin — but the presence of round bubbles almost always indicates glass. Some natural stones (like enhydro quartz) do contain moving water bubbles, but these are elongated or angular, not perfectly round. Requires a loupe; the naked eye usually cannot resolve the smallest bubbles.

Best stones for this test: Transparent stones sold as clear quartz, citrine, topaz, or diamond simulants. Less useful for opaque stones where you cannot see inside.

Source: GIA Gem Reference Guide — inclusions in natural vs. synthetic; mindat.org.

🌈 Test 5: Color Banding & Zoning

The science behind it: Natural color in minerals forms unevenly because crystal growth happens over geological time under changing conditions. Genuine amethyst shows subtle color zoning — deeper purple at the tips, lighter near the base, with natural gradation. Synthetic quartz grown in a lab colors uniformly and looks "flat" or overly saturated. Dyed stones concentrate pigment in surface cracks and fissures rather than throughout.

Real vs fake at a glance:

SignalGenuineFake (glass / plastic)
Color distributionUneven zoning, natural gradationUniform, flat, "too perfect"
Where color sitsThroughout the crystal, following growth structureDyed: concentrated in cracks/fissures; synthetic: perfectly even
SaturationSubtle variation, lighter and darker zonesSingle flat tone, often over-saturated

How to do it:

  1. Look at the stone under daylight (not yellow indoor light, which distorts color).
  2. Examine whether the color is uniform or shows natural lighter/darker zones — genuine amethyst, citrine, and fluorite almost always show some variation.
  3. For suspected dye: check cracks and chips — dye pools in surface fissures and looks more intense there.

Limitations: Some high-quality natural stones are genuinely uniform in color, and some synthetic stones are deliberately made with simulated zoning. So uniform color is a flag, not a verdict — combine with other tests. Dye tests can fade over time; a cotton swab dampened with acetone (for non-quartz stones) can lift surface dye, but this is mildly destructive.

Best stones for this test: Amethyst (most classic case), citrine, fluorite, agate, rose quartz, lapis lazuli. Not useful for naturally uniform stones or opaque dyed stones without cracks to inspect.

Source: GIA Gem Reference Guide — quartz color zoning; mindat.org — amethyst color cause (iron + irradiation).

⚖️ Test 3: Specific Gravity (Heft) Test

The science behind it: Specific gravity (SG) measures density relative to water. Natural quartz has an SG of 2.65 — meaning a given volume of quartz weighs 2.65 times the same volume of water. Most common glass falls around 2.4-2.6, plastic around 1.0-1.5. Holding two stones of the same size, the denser genuine crystal feels noticeably heavier in the hand ("good heft"), while plastic feels suspiciously light.

Real vs fake at a glance:

SignalGenuineFake (glass / plastic)
Weight in hand for same sizeSubstantial — "good heft" (SG 2.65 quartz)Lighter (glass ~2.5, plastic ~1.2)
Ratio to water displacement2.65 (quartz), 3.3 (jadeite)2.4-2.6 (glass), 1.0-1.5 (plastic)
Tapping two same-size beadsDenser, deeper tapHollower tap

How to do it:

  1. Compare the suspected stone against a known genuine stone of similar size (or against a glass marble of the same diameter).
  2. Weigh them in your palm alternately — a genuine quartz bead feels about 10% heavier than an equal-size glass bead.
  3. For precision: weigh the stone dry, then suspended in water, and divide — but for most home checks, hand heft is enough.

Limitations: The difference between quartz (2.65) and glass (2.4-2.6) is small enough that hand heft alone is unreliable without a reference object of the same size. Plastic (1.0-1.5) is obvious, but high-quality glass imitations can feel similar. A precision jewelry scale with water displacement is needed for an accurate SG reading.

Best stones for this test: All stones where there is a meaningful density gap — especially distinguishing plastic imitations of any stone, and jadeite (SG 3.3, distinctly heavier than the quartz or serpentine often substituted).

Source: mindat.org — mineral specific gravity data; GIA Gem Reference Guide.

🔨 Test 2: Hardness / Scratch Test

The science behind it: The Mohs scale ranks minerals by scratch resistance from 1 (talc) to 10 (diamond). Genuine quartz sits at Mohs 7, meaning it can scratch ordinary window glass (about Mohs 5.5) and steel (around Mohs 6). Glass imitations, being softer or equally soft, cannot reliably scratch true glass. The test exploits this hardness gap.

Real vs fake at a glance:

SignalGenuineFake (glass / plastic)
Scratching glassQuartz (Mohs 7) scratches glass easilyGlass (Mohs 5.5) cannot scratch glass
Being scratched by steelSteel (Mohs 6) cannot scratch quartzSteel can scratch glass imitation
Edge after scratchLeaves a clear grooveNo mark, or only crumbles

How to do it:

  1. Take a piece of ordinary window glass (a glass jar you do not mind damaging, never good glassware).
  2. Press a sharp edge or point of the stone firmly and drag it across the glass.
  3. A genuine quartz leaves a visible scratch groove; a glass imitation leaves no mark or only a faint streak that rubs off.

Limitations: This is a destructive test — it damages both the stone (edge) and the glass. Never use it on finished jewelry, polished pieces, or stones you want to keep pristine. It does not work for genuine soft stones (malachite Mohs 3.5-4, calcite Mohs 3, turquoise Mohs 5-6) which legitimately cannot scratch glass. Use only as a confirmation on quartz-family rough or tumbled stones.

Best stones for this test: Quartz family (Mohs 7), topaz (Mohs 8), jadeite (Mohs 6.5-7). Not suitable for soft stones, jewelry, or polished specimens.

Source: mindat.org — Mohs hardness scale; USGS Mineral Database — quartz hardness.

🟣 Test 6: UV Light (Fluorescence) Test

The science behind it: Some minerals fluoresce — emit visible light — under ultraviolet (UV) radiation, due to trace impurities called activators. Natural fluorite often fluoresces blue or purple; genuine scapolite glows yellow-orange; some natural quartz shows weak fluorescence. The fluorescence pattern of a natural stone differs from glass (usually inert) and from some synthetics (which may fluoresce differently). A 365nm UV flashlight reveals this.

Real vs fake at a glance:

SignalGenuineFake (glass / plastic)
Under 365nm UV lightSpecific fluorescence (e.g., fluorite → blue/purple)Glass usually inert; some synthetics fluoresce abnormally
Fluorescence patternPatchy, follows natural growth zonesUniform or absent (glass), or too uniform (some synthetics)
Color of glowSpecies-specific (scapolite yellow, fluorite blue)None, or wrong color for the claimed species

How to do it:

  1. Use a 365nm UV flashlight (about $10-15 online; the shorter 395nm "blacklight" is less precise).
  2. Darken the room as much as possible.
  3. Shine the UV on the stone and observe any glow — note the color and whether it is patchy or uniform.

Limitations: Not all minerals fluoresce — many genuine stones (including most clear quartz) show no reaction, so a negative result does not mean fake. Fluorescence also varies even within a single mineral species depending on impurities. This test is most useful as confirmation for known fluorescent species (fluorite, scapolite, some calcite, some amber). Requires a UV light source and a dark room.

Best stones for this test: Fluorite (reliable blue/purple), scapolite, amber (fluoresces blue-green), some calcite. Not useful as a general test for non-fluorescent species like clear quartz.

Source: mindat.org — mineral fluorescence data; GIA Gem Reference Guide.

Mineral Data Reference (Mohs Hardness, Specific Gravity, Refractive Index)

The numbers behind the tests — these are fixed mineralogical facts, not opinion. Use them to verify what the home tests suggest.

StoneMohs hardnessSpecific gravityRefractive indexFormulaCommon imitation
Amethyst72.651.544SiO₂ (silicon dioxide)Synthetic (lab-grown) purple quartz
Citrine72.651.544SiO₂ (silicon dioxide)Heat-treated amethyst sold as citrine (very common, often undisclosed)
Turquoise5–62.6–2.9~1.62CuAl₆(PO₄)₄(OH)₈·4H₂O (hydrated copper aluminum phosphate)Howlite dyed blue (the most common fake)
Lapis Lazuli5–5.52.7–2.9~1.50Lazurite + calcite + pyrite (a rock, not a single mineral)Dyed jasper or howlite
Moldavite5–5.52.3–2.4~1.49–1.51SiO₂-rich natural glass (tektite — formed by meteorite impact)Green bottle glass (most common fake)
Malachite3.5–43.6–4.0~1.86Cu₂CO₃(OH)₂ (copper carbonate hydroxide)Plastic / resin
Jadeite6.5–7~3.3 (distinctly heavier than substitutes)~1.66NaAlSi₂O₆ (sodium aluminum silicate — a pyroxene)Dyed quartz (most common fake for green jade)
Obsidian5–5.5~2.4~1.50Natural volcanic glass (~70-75% SiO₂, amorphous)Industrial glass (the hardest to distinguish — same material family)
Clear Quartz72.651.544SiO₂ (silicon dioxide)Ordinary glass (most common)
Rose Quartz72.651.544–1.553SiO₂ (silicon dioxide)Glass dyed pink

Mineral data: GIA Gem Reference Guide, mindat.org, USGS Mineral Database. SG = specific gravity; RI = refractive index.

The 10 Most Faked Crystals

These are the ten crystals most often sold as fakes, ranked roughly by how commonly they are imitated. Each entry gives the mineral profile, what gets faked, the key tests that catch the imitations, and the price range that helps you spot a "too good to be true" listing. For amethyst and clear quartz — the two most faked — there are dedicated deep-dive guides; the entries here hit the essentials.

Amethyst

Mineral data: Formula SiO₂ (silicon dioxide) · Mohs 7 · Specific gravity 2.65 · Refractive index 1.544 · Color cause: Iron + natural irradiation. Primary origins: Brazil, Uruguay, Zambia, Mexico.

What gets faked:

  • Synthetic (lab-grown) purple quartz
  • Glass dyed purple
  • Heat-treated low-grade amethyst sold as "citrine"

How to tell Amethyst is real — the key tests:

  1. Color zoning is the headline test — natural amethyst shows subtle lighter/darker purple gradients, especially concentrated at crystal tips. Synthetic amethyst looks uniformly saturated and "too perfect."
  2. Inclusions under 10x loupe: natural amethyst has needle-like rutile, two-phase gas-liquid inclusions, or "fingerprints." Synthetic amethyst often shows "breadcrumb" inclusions from lab seed-crystal growth.
  3. UV fluorescence: synthetic amethyst frequently shows a blue fluorescence under shortwave UV; natural amethyst is usually inert or shows weak reddish-brown.
  4. Pricing sanity check: a large "natural amethyst" geode at the price of a tumbled stone is almost certainly dyed or reconstituted.

Price sanity check: Tumbled stones $2-8; beads $0.50-3 each; small geodes $15-60; large cathedral geodes $100-2000+ depending on size and quality

Source: GIA Gem Reference Guide — quartz; mindat.org — amethyst (Fe³⁺ + irradiation color cause).

Read the full Amethyst meaning · Shop Amethyst

Citrine

Mineral data: Formula SiO₂ (silicon dioxide) · Mohs 7 · Specific gravity 2.65 · Refractive index 1.544 · Color cause: Iron (Fe³⁺) + heat. Primary origins: Brazil, Madagascar, Russia, Spain.

What gets faked:

  • Heat-treated amethyst sold as citrine (very common, often undisclosed)
  • Glass dyed yellow/orange
  • Synthetic quartz

How to tell Citrine is real — the key tests:

  1. Color tells the story: natural citrine is pale yellow to soft amber. The vivid "burnt orange" citrine ubiquitous in shops is almost always heat-treated amethyst — disclosure is the issue, not the treatment itself.
  2. Heat-treated amethyst often retains a subtle reddish-brown undertone and may show faint color zoning remnants from the original purple.
  3. Reputable sellers disclose heat treatment; the FTC requires it. "Natural citrine" priced the same as amethyst is a flag.
  4. Glass dyed yellow shows dye concentrations in cracks under a loupe.

Price sanity check: Tumbled $2-6; beads $0.50-3; small points $5-15; natural untreated citrine commands a premium

Source: GIA Gem Reference Guide — citrine color cause (Fe³⁺ + heat).

Read the full Citrine meaning · Shop Citrine

Turquoise

Mineral data: Formula CuAl₆(PO₄)₄(OH)₈·4H₂O (hydrated copper aluminum phosphate) · Mohs 5–6 · Specific gravity 2.6–2.9 · Refractive index ~1.62 · Color cause: Copper (blue), iron (green). Primary origins: Iran, USA (Southwest), China, Mexico, Egypt.

What gets faked:

  • Howlite dyed blue (the most common fake)
  • Plastic / resin (block turquoise, "reconstituted")
  • Magnesite dyed
  • Glass

How to tell Turquoise is real — the key tests:

  1. Matrix inspection: genuine turquoise has a natural matrix (host rock veins — brown, black, or golden). Dyed howlite has dark veining that looks "spiderweb" but the base color is wrong (too uniform blue, matrix too regular).
  2. The "too perfect" test: genuine turquoise varies in color across a piece; dyed howlite and plastic are uniform.
  3. Specific gravity: plastic block turquoise feels suspiciously light; genuine turquoise has a heftier feel.
  4. Price flag: large "natural turquoise" beads at $1-2 each are almost certainly howlite or plastic.
  5. Stabilization disclosure: most genuine turquoise on the market is "stabilized" (resin-impregnated for durability) — reputable sellers disclose this; it is legitimate, but should not be sold as "untreated."

Price sanity check: Genuine tumbled $5-20; beads $1-5; untreated Sleeping Beauty grade $20-100+; fakes often $1-3

Source: GIA Gem Reference Guide — turquoise; mindat.org — turquoise formula and origins.

Read the full Turquoise meaning · Shop Turquoise

Lapis Lazuli

Mineral data: Formula Lazurite + calcite + pyrite (a rock, not a single mineral) · Mohs 5–5.5 · Specific gravity 2.7–2.9 · Refractive index ~1.50 · Color cause: Lazurite sulfur-bearing radicals (blue), pyrite (gold flecks). Primary origins: Afghanistan (primary, finest), Chile, Russia, Pakistan.

What gets faked:

  • Dyed jasper or howlite
  • Synthetic lapis (Gilson)
  • Sodalite sold as lapis
  • Dyed marble

How to tell Lapis Lazuli is real — the key tests:

  1. Pyrite flecks: genuine lapis has real gold-colored pyrite flecks scattered irregularly — they look metallic and catch light. Fakes either have no pyrite, or pyrite painted/printed in too-uniform dots.
  2. Calcite white veining: genuine lapis often shows white calcite streaks. Perfectly uniform deep-blue lapis with no calcite or pyrite is suspicious.
  3. The "too blue" test: dyed howlite and jasper achieve an unnaturally uniform royal blue; genuine lapis has natural variation.
  4. Sodalite substitution: sodalite is a related blue mineral but lacks pyrite and has a different (often more violet) tone — sold cheaply as "lapis."

Price sanity check: Tumbled $3-12; beads $0.50-4; high-grade Afghan with pyrite $10-50 per piece

Source: GIA Gem Reference Guide — lapis lazuli; mindat.org — lazurite.

Read the full Lapis Lazuli meaning · Shop Lapis Lazuli

Moldavite

Mineral data: Formula SiO₂-rich natural glass (tektite — formed by meteorite impact) · Mohs 5–5.5 · Specific gravity 2.3–2.4 · Refractive index ~1.49–1.51 · Color cause: Iron and other impurities in natural impact glass. Primary origins: Czech Republic only (Bohemia & Moravia) — the Moldau River valley.

What gets faked:

  • Green bottle glass (most common fake)
  • Synthetic green glass with etched texture
  • Other green tektites sold as moldavite
  • Lab-created imitations

How to tell Moldavite is real — the key tests:

  1. Origin is everything: genuine moldavite comes only from the Czech Republic (the Moldau valley). A seller cannot name the source, or offers a huge piece cheaply, is a red flag.
  2. Lechatelierite bubbles and flow texture: genuine moldavite has distinctive sculpted, wrinkled "schuss" texture from its formation, and often contains small bubbles.
  3. Price flag is extreme: genuine moldavite has become expensive (mined-out supply); large "moldavite" at bargain prices is almost certainly bottle glass.
  4. UV test: some genuine moldavite shows no fluorescence (varies), but green bottle glass often fluoresces differently under UV.
  5. Color: genuine moldavite is a distinctive forest-green to olive; bottle-glass fakes are often too bright or too uniform.

Price sanity check: Genuine small pieces $20-80/gram (prices have risen sharply); medium $100-500; fakes $5-20 for a "large piece"

Source: mindat.org — moldavite tektite; GIA Gem Reference Guide — tektites.

Read the full Moldavite meaning · Shop Moldavite

Malachite

Mineral data: Formula Cu₂CO₃(OH)₂ (copper carbonate hydroxide) · Mohs 3.5–4 · Specific gravity 3.6–4.0 · Refractive index ~1.86 · Color cause: Copper. Primary origins: DRC (Congo), Russia, Zambia, Namibia, Australia.

What gets faked:

  • Plastic / resin
  • Dyed howlite or magnesite
  • Printed vinyl on stone (cheap beads)
  • Synthetic

How to tell Malachite is real — the key tests:

  1. Banding is the signature: genuine malachite has natural, irregular green banding (light and dark green waves) — never perfectly repeating patterns. Plastic and printed fakes have too-regular bands.
  2. Specific gravity is a giveaway: genuine malachite is dense (SG 3.6-4.0) — it feels heavy for its size. Plastic feels light.
  3. Hardness: malachite is genuinely soft (Mohs 3.5-4) — a copper coin or fingernail can scratch it gently. (Conduct gently to avoid damage.)
  4. Safety note: malachite is copper-bearing — keep it dry and away from acids, and do not make "malachite water" for drinking (copper compounds are water-soluble and toxic).
  5. Color: genuine malachite greens are deep and varied; fakes are often a single flat green with painted bands.

Price sanity check: Tumbled $3-10; beads $0.50-4; banded quality $5-30 per piece

Source: GIA / USGS — copper-bearing mineralogy; mindat.org — malachite.

Read the full Malachite meaning · Shop Malachite

Jadeite

Mineral data: Formula NaAlSi₂O₆ (sodium aluminum silicate — a pyroxene) · Mohs 6.5–7 · Specific gravity ~3.3 (distinctly heavier than substitutes) · Refractive index ~1.66 · Color cause: Chromium (emerald green "imperial jade"), iron (other greens, lavender). Primary origins: Myanmar (primary source for gem jadeite), Guatemala, Russia, Japan.

What gets faked:

  • Dyed quartz (most common fake for green jade)
  • Serpentine (a softer, cheaper green stone)
  • Nephrite (a different, legitimate jade mineral — disclosure issue)
  • Glass and plastic
  • Dyed 'jade' of unknown stone

How to tell Jadeite is real — the key tests:

  1. Specific gravity is the strongest home test: genuine jadeite (SG 3.3) feels distinctly heavy — heavier than quartz (2.65) of the same size. Most substitutes (serpentine, dyed quartz) feel lighter.
  2. The "coin tap" sound: genuine jadeite produces a clear, resonant chime when tapped with a coin (its dense structure); serpentine and glass sound dull.
  3. Cold to touch: jadeite has high thermal conductivity and feels cool.
  4. Color: dyed green "jade" often shows dye concentrations in cracks and looks unnaturally uniform. Genuine jadeite color comes from chromium and varies naturally.
  5. For high-value jadeite (bangles, carvings claiming imperial grade), a gemological lab certificate is essential — home tests cannot distinguish treated dyed jadeite from natural.

Price sanity check: Vast range: ordinary carved "jade" $5-30; genuine jadeite bangles $50-500; imperial jadeite $1000-100,000+; fakes often under $20

Source: GIA Gem Reference Guide — jadeite vs. nephrite; mindat.org — jadeite.

Read the full Jadeite meaning · Shop Jadeite

Obsidian

Mineral data: Formula Natural volcanic glass (~70-75% SiO₂, amorphous) · Mohs 5–5.5 · Specific gravity ~2.4 · Refractive index ~1.50 · Color cause: Tiny mineral inclusions (black), cristobalite (snowflake patterns), thin-film interference (rainbow). Primary origins: Mexico, USA, Iceland, Italy, Ethiopia, Japan.

What gets faked:

  • Industrial glass (the hardest to distinguish — same material family)
  • Molded black glass beads
  • Fake 'snowflake' obsidian (painted white spots on black glass)

How to tell Obsidian is real — the key tests:

  1. Obsidian is itself a natural glass — so distinguishing it from manufactured glass is genuinely difficult. This is the honest caveat.
  2. Inclusions are the key: genuine obsidian often has natural mineral inclusions, microcrystals (cristobalite in snowflake obsidian), or chatoyant bands. Industrial glass tends to be too uniform or have manufacturing bubbles.
  3. Snowflake obsidian: the "snowflakes" are natural cristobalite crystals — irregular, fern-like. Painted-on white spots on fake snowflake obsidian look too regular and sit on the surface.
  4. Rainbow obsidian: genuine rainbow obsidian shows concentric color bands (from thin-film interference) visible when lit from the right angle — difficult to fake.
  5. Price: obsidian is genuinely affordable, so fakes are less common than for amethyst or turquoise — but "too perfect" mirror-polished uniform black glass can still be manufactured.

Price sanity check: Tumbled $2-6; beads $0.30-2; mirror-polished spheres $8-30; fakes similar prices but uniform perfection

Source: USGS — volcanic glass; mindat.org — obsidian.

Read the full Obsidian meaning · Shop Obsidian

Clear Quartz

Mineral data: Formula SiO₂ (silicon dioxide) · Mohs 7 · Specific gravity 2.65 · Refractive index 1.544 · Color cause: Colorless (no impurities). Primary origins: Brazil, Madagascar, Arkansas (USA), Himalayas.

What gets faked:

  • Ordinary glass (most common)
  • Reconstituted quartz (quartz powder melted with glass)
  • Synthetic lab-grown quartz
  • Lead crystal glass ("crystal" naming trap)

How to tell Clear Quartz is real — the key tests:

  1. Bubbles under 10x loupe are the #1 signal — glass has round air bubbles; genuine clear quartz has irregular inclusions (needles, veils, mineral flecks) or is inclusion-free.
  2. Hardness test: genuine clear quartz (Mohs 7) scratches glass; glass imitations cannot.
  3. The "crystal quartz" naming trap — the glass industry calls leaded glass "crystal" (lead crystal, crystal glass). "Crystal quartz" sold cheaply is often glass. Natural quartz is properly called "quartz."
  4. Three quality tiers to distinguish: natural clear quartz (has minor inclusions), clarified/reconstituted (too perfect, may show faint flow lines), and pure glass (bubbles, no quartz characteristics).
  5. Double refraction: genuine quartz shows slight doubling when you look through it at a sharp angle (text appears doubled); glass does not — but this needs magnification to see.

Price sanity check: Small tumbled $1-4; small points $3-10; clusters $10-50; large cathedral points $30-300; museum-grade clusters $200-2000+

Source: mindat.org — quartz; GIA Gem Reference Guide — quartz RI 1.544, birefringence 0.009.

Read the full Clear Quartz meaning · Shop Clear Quartz

Rose Quartz

Mineral data: Formula SiO₂ (silicon dioxide) · Mohs 7 · Specific gravity 2.65 · Refractive index 1.544–1.553 · Color cause: Titanium, manganese + irradiation. Primary origins: Brazil, Madagascar, South Africa, India, USA.

What gets faked:

  • Glass dyed pink
  • Plastic beads (cheap bracelets)
  • Synthetic quartz
  • Reconstituted (pink quartz powder + glass)

How to tell Rose Quartz is real — the key tests:

  1. Color variation: genuine rose quartz shows subtle pink variation and often a slightly cloudy appearance. Glass and plastic look uniform and clear.
  2. Hardness test: genuine rose quartz (Mohs 7) scratches glass; plastic and glass do not.
  3. Temperature: genuine rose quartz feels cool and stays cool; plastic warms immediately.
  4. The "too cheap bracelet" test: a 10mm bead rose quartz bracelet for $2-3 on a marketplace is very likely plastic or glass — genuine rose quartz beads cost more to cut and string.
  5. Asterism (star effect): some genuine rose quartz shows a six-rayed star when cut en cabochon and lit — a strong authenticity signal.

Price sanity check: Tumbled $2-6; beads $0.30-2; raw chunks $5-15; fakes similar prices

Source: mindat.org — rose quartz (Ti/Mn + irradiation color cause).

When to Use Professional Tools

The tools below cover the tests in this guide. Most are inexpensive; the lab certificate is reserved for high-value specimens.

  • A 10x jeweler's loupe is the most valuable tool across all ten stones. It reveals bubbles in glass (amethyst, citrine, clear quartz, rose quartz), dye concentrations in cracks (turquoise, lapis, malachite), the texture of pyrite in lapis, and the natural inclusions that separate genuine stones from manufactured perfection.
  • A Mohs hardness reference confirms the quartz family (amethyst, citrine, clear quartz, rose quartz all at Mohs 7) by their ability to scratch glass. Use only on rough stones — never on jewelry or polished pieces.
  • A 365nm UV flashlight helps with a few stones on this list: synthetic amethyst (blue fluorescence), fluorite species, and some amber. For most of the ten, it is a secondary tool rather than a primary one.
  • A gemological refractometer measures refractive index precisely, distinguishing quartz (1.544) from glass (1.50) and jadeite (1.66) from its substitutes. A professional tool — most buyers will rely on a gemologist rather than own one.
  • For high-value stones on this list — jadeite bangles, moldavite over a few hundred dollars, untreated Sleeping Beauty turquoise, large fine amethyst geodes — a gemological laboratory certificate is the only definitive answer. Home tests narrow the odds; a lab report settles them.

For high-value specimens (jadeite, imperial-grade amethyst, moldavite, large geodes), a gemological laboratory certificate is the only definitive answer — home tests narrow the odds, they do not replace a professional report.

Frequently Asked Questions About Real vs Fake Crystals

Which crystals are most commonly faked?

The most commonly faked crystals are, in rough order: amethyst (synthetic and dyed glass), citrine (heat-treated amethyst sold as natural), turquoise (dyed howlite and plastic), lapis lazuli (dyed jasper and sodalite), moldavite (green bottle glass), malachite (plastic and printed vinyl), jadeite (dyed quartz and serpentine), obsidian (industrial glass, though harder to detect), clear quartz (glass and reconstituted), and rose quartz (glass and plastic). These ten cover the great majority of fakes a buyer will encounter, because each is popular, visually copyable, and — for the high grades — valuable enough to make substitution profitable.

How can you tell if a crystal is fake?

Apply the general tests (bubbles, temperature, hardness, price, naming) plus the one species-specific test for the stone. For turquoise, look for natural matrix and color variation. For lapis, look for real pyrite flecks. For jadeite, feel the density (it should be distinctly heavy). For moldavite, confirm Czech origin. For any transparent stone (amethyst, citrine, clear quartz, rose quartz), check for round bubbles under a 10x loupe — bubbles mean glass. Run the tests together; a genuine stone passes several, a fake fails several.

What is the most faked crystal?

Turquoise and amethyst lead the list. Turquoise is faked constantly because genuine untreated turquoise is expensive and dyed howlite copies its color closely — the "turquoise" in much inexpensive jewelry is dyed howlite or plastic. Amethyst is faked constantly because it is the most popular crystal and synthetic amethyst (chemically identical) plus dyed glass fill the market. Moldavite deserves a special mention: because genuine moldavite is rare and has become expensive, the fake rate is extremely high — large "moldavite" pieces at low prices are almost always green bottle glass.

Is fake turquoise common?

Yes, extremely common. Genuine untreated turquoise is relatively expensive (especially fine grades like Sleeping Beauty), and dyed howlite — a cheap white mineral that takes blue dye well — copies turquoise's color closely enough to fool casual buyers. The result is that a large share of "turquoise" in inexpensive jewelry and beads is actually dyed howlite, plastic "block turquoise" (resin with turquoise dust), or magnesite. The tests: look for natural matrix (host-rock veining, never too uniform), color variation (genuine turquoise varies; dyed howlite is uniform), and the right density (plastic feels light). A reputable seller discloses stabilization, which is legitimate, and names the origin.

Where can I buy genuine crystals that are not fake?

Look for sellers who do three things across all these heavily faked stones: name each stone by its actual mineral species (not vague terms), disclose treatments (dyed, heat-treated, stabilized, lab-created), and state a specific origin. Beyond that, check for mineral data availability, a clear return policy, and real photographs showing natural variation between pieces. The crystal collection on this site names stones and sources precisely because these are the stones where fakes concentrate — naming and sourcing are how you verify rather than trust.

How much do real crystals cost vs fakes?

Fakes are dramatically cheaper than genuine stones, especially for high-value species. A few ratios: genuine moldavite starts around $20-80 per gram, while green-glass fakes sell for $5-20 for a large piece. Genuine untreated turquoise beads run $1-5 each, while dyed howlite beads cost cents. Genuine jadeite bangles start around $50 and fine imperial jadeite reaches thousands, while dyed-quartz "jade" bangles sell under $20. The absolute price matters less than the ratio to the species' market range — a price far below the established range for that stone and grade is the strongest pre-purchase warning.

Are lab-created crystals considered fake?

Lab-created stones are genuine in composition (synthetic amethyst is real SiO₂), but they are not natural — they were grown in a factory rather than mined. Whether they count as "fake" depends on disclosure. Sold as "lab-created" or "synthetic," they are legitimate and often beautiful. Sold as natural without disclosure, they are misleading and against FTC guidelines. For the stones on this list — especially amethyst, citrine, and clear quartz — always check the listing for "lab-created," "synthetic," or "heat-treated," and ask the seller directly if you want a natural specimen.

Are fake crystals dangerous?

Most fake crystals are not health-hazardous — they are glass, plastic, or dyed stone, which are inert. The real cost is financial and emotional: paying for something you did not receive. A few mineral-specific notes apply across this list: malachite is copper-bearing, so "malachite water" (soaking and drinking) is not safe. Dyed stones (turquoise, lapis, agate) can bleed pigment onto skin or fabric. Beyond these specific cases, the concern with fakes is honesty, not toxicity.

Can home tests spot all fakes?

Home tests do not catch every fake — they catch the obvious ones. Glass sold as quartz, plastic sold as turquoise, and dyed howlite sold as lapis are easily filtered with a $10 loupe and the species-specific tests. But sophisticated imitations — well-made synthetic quartz, expertly reconstituted material, high-quality dyed stones — can pass several home tests. For valuable specimens (jadeite, moldavite, untreated turquoise, fine amethyst geodes), a gemological laboratory report is the only definitive answer. Think of home tests as a strong first filter that handles the bulk of the market, not a final verdict on expensive pieces.

Why is moldavite so often faked?

Moldavite is faked so often because genuine supply is scarce and shrinking, while demand has risen sharply. Moldavite is a tektite — natural glass formed by a meteorite impact about 15 million years ago — and it is found only in the Czech Republic, in the Moldau River valley. The deposits are largely mined out, which has driven prices up steeply. That combination of high price, limited supply, and a material (green glass) that visually approximates moldavite creates a strong incentive to substitute. The result is that large "moldavite" pieces at bargain prices are almost always green bottle glass. The tests: confirm Czech origin, look for natural lechatelierite bubbles and sculpted texture, and treat price as a primary signal.

Is dyed howlite always sold as turquoise?

Very often. Howlite is a soft, white, porous mineral that takes blue dye exceptionally well, which makes it the most common substitute for turquoise. It is also sometimes dyed to imitate lapis or red coral. There is nothing wrong with howlite itself — it is a legitimate stone — but when dyed blue and sold as turquoise without disclosure, it is misleading. The tests: dyed howlite usually has a uniform royal-blue color (genuine turquoise varies), dark veining that looks too regular, and no genuine matrix. A reputable seller labels dyed howlite honestly rather than passing it off as turquoise.

This guide covers the stones most often sold as imitations. Home tests help you spot common fakes, but for valuable specimens (jadeite, moldavite, untreated turquoise), a gemological lab report is the gold standard. Crystal meanings and energetic properties are separate from authenticity.

How to Buy Genuine Crystals (Without Getting Fooled)

Across all ten of these heavily faked stones, the same seller signals separate reputable sources from those moving imitations. If you are shopping for any stone on this list, these five checks come first.

Five Signs of a Reputable Seller

  • Naming transparency. The listing names the actual mineral species — "Natural Turquoise," "Lapis Lazuli," "Jadeite" — not vague terms. Treatments are disclosed: dyed howlite sold as turquoise, heat-treated amethyst sold as citrine, stabilized turquoise, dyed quartz sold as jade. A seller who names stones correctly is signaling they know the difference.
  • Locality disclosure. The listing states a specific origin — Afghanistan for lapis, the Czech Republic for moldavite, Iran or the American Southwest for turquoise, Myanmar for jadeite. Origin matters for these stones because it affects value and because some origins (Czech-only moldavite) are part of how you confirm authenticity.
  • Mineral data availability. The seller provides Mohs hardness, specific gravity, or refractive index — or links to it. Sellers who understand mineralogy bother; sellers moving fakes usually do not. The data also lets you cross-check the home tests.
  • Clear return policy. A 30-day return policy shifts risk to the seller and gives you time to test the stone at home. Sellers who move fakes resist returns because returned fakes expose them.
  • Real photographs of the actual piece. Natural stones vary — each turquoise bead has its own matrix, each lapis chunk its own pyrite pattern. Mass-produced fakes look identical across listings. Real photos showing natural variation are a positive sign; uniform catalog shots are not.

How Earthward Approaches This

At Earthward, we name each stone by its mineral species, state the origin where we know it, and disclose treatments honestly. The stones on this list — amethyst, clear quartz, turquoise, lapis, and the rest — are exactly where fakes concentrate, so we are deliberate about naming and sourcing. "Return to what's real" means we tell you what you are holding, with its natural variation and inclusions, rather than hiding behind a vague label.
